6144.history: Interned at Cartagena, Spain on 27 November 1942. Returned to France on 29 November 1945. Stricken on 1 February 1950.

6145.history: Scuttled at Toulon on 27 November 1942 to prevent her capture by the Germans.

6146.history: Seized by the British on 3 July 1940 and transferred to the Free French on 27 July. Decommissioned 6 December 1954.

6147.history: Disarmed at Oran in October 1942. Scuttled at Oran on 9 November 1942. Raised by the Allies and stricken in 1944

6148.history: Disarmed at Oran in October 1942. Scuttled at Oran on 9 November 1942. Raised by the Allies. Stricken 18 February 1946.

6149.built_by: At.&Ch de la Loire (St. Nazaire, France)
6149.history: On 8 November 1942 she sortied from Oran to attack the British landing forces (Operation "Torch"). Off Arzew she was detected and attacked by the British destroyer HMS Westcott, being sunk by DCs with no survivors.

6150.built_by: Swan Hunter and Wigham Richardson Ltd. (Wallsend-on-Tyne, U.K.)
6150.history: Disarmed at Fort de France, Martinique in November 1940. Stricken 19 September 1947.

6151.history: Sunk on 9 November 1940 off Libreville, Gabon by her Free French sistership Savorgnan de Brazza.

6152.built_by: Ch Maritime du Sud Ost (Bordeaux, France)
6152.history: Rejoined the Allies on 23 November 1942. Stricken 26 March 1958.

6153.built_by: Ch Maritime du Sud Ost (Bordeaux, France)
6153.history: Seized by the British on 3 July 1940 while at Portsmouth. Transferred to the Free French on 24 September 1940. Stricken 20 March 1957.

6154.history: Damaged and beached off Madagascar on 5 May 1942. Salvaged by the Allies. Finally stricken on 19 October 1948.

6155.history: Rigault de Genouilly (Capitaine De Fregate Louis Georges Emile Frossard) was torpedoed and sunk 6 nautical miles north of Matifou, Algeria by the British submarine HMS Pandora.
commanders: Capitaine De Fregate Louis Georges Emile Frossard, FR (18 Jan 1940 - 4 Jul 1940)

6156.built_by: Ch Maritime du Sud Ost (Bordeaux, France)
6156.history: Scuttled at My-Tho, Indo-China on 10 March 1945.

6157.built_by: Ch Maritime du Sud Ost (Bordeaux, France)
6157.history: Scuttled at Toulon on 27 November 1942 to prevent her capture by the Germans.

6158.history: Rejoined the Allies on 8 November 1942. Stricken 23 November 1959.

6159.history: Disarmed on 31 October 1940 at Port-Lyautey, Morocco. Stricken 14 October 1941.

6160.history: Scuttled at Toulon on 27 November 1942 to prevent her capture by the Germans. Raised by the Italians on 5 February 1943 but not repaired. Finally stricken on 22 November 1956.

6161.history: Scuttled off Can Tho, Indo-China on 10 March 1945. Raised on 19 June 1957 and broken up for scrap.

6162.history: Disarmed at Port-Lyautey, Morocco on 31 October 1940. Finally stricken on 21 December 1956

6163.history: Seized by the British on 3 July 1940 while in Falmouth. Transferred to the free French but not rearmed. Sunk on 14 April 1941 during a German air raid on Falmouth. Raised and found beyond repair, finally stricken on 15 June 1941.

6164.history: Disarmed at Port-Lyautey, Morocco on 28 October 1940. Stricken 14 October 1941.

6165.history: Scuttled at Lorient on 18 June 1940 to prevent her capture by the Germans.

6166.history: Seized at Portsmouth on 3 July 1940 by the British. Transferred to the Free French and used as a base ship.

6167.built_by: F.&Ch de la Mediterranee (La Seyne, France)
6167.history: Seized at Portsmouth on 3 July 1940 by the British. Transferred to the Free French and used as a base ship. Finally stricken on 13 October 1949.

6168.history: Seized by the British at Plymouth on 3 July 1940. Transferred to the Free French but not active during the war. Returned to Cherbourg on 27 September 1945. Finally stricken on 22 November 1956.

6169.built_by: F.&Ch de la Mediterranee (La Seyne, France)
6169.history: Used as a base ship at Dakar.

6170.history: Seized by the British at Plymouth on 3 July 1940.

6171.history: Seized at Portsmouth by the British on 3 July 1940. Transferred to the Free French and used as base ship. Returned to Cherbourg on 6 September 1945. Further fate unknown.

6172.history: Disarmed at Bizerte, Tunisia on 31 October 1940. Captured by the Italians on 8 December 1942. Scuttled by the Italians in July 1943. Finally stricken on 12 August 1947.

6173.history: Scuttled at Toulon on 27 November 1942 to prevent her capture by the Germans. Refloated by the Italians. Captured by the Germans in September 1943 and used by them as minesweeper, being renamed M 6060. Further fate unknown.

6174.built_by: At.&Ch de la Loire (St. Nazaire, France)
6174.history: Sunk on 30 April 1944 off Cape Varella, French Indochina in position 13º02'N, 109º28'E by the American submarine USS Flasher (SS 249).

6175.built_by: At.&Ch de la Loire (St. Nazaire, France)
6175.history: Sunk off Brest by a Magnetic mine on 18 June 1940.

6178.built_by: At.&Ch de la Loire (St. Nazaire, France)
6178.history: Scuttled at Toulon on 27 November 1942 to prevent her capture by the Germans. Raised by the Italians on 17 July 1943. Captured by the Germans in September 1943 and renamed SG 26. Captured by the Allies in Genoa, Italy. Further fate unknown.

6180.history: Scuttled at Toulon on 27 November 1942 to prevent her capture by the Germans. Raised by the Italians and renamed FR 56. Camptured by the Germans and renamed M 6020. Further fate unknown.

6181.history: Scuttled at Brest on 18 June 1940 to prevent her capture by the Germans.

6182.history: Captured by the Allies after landings in North Africa. Decommissioned in 1944.

6183.history: Seized by the British on 3 July 1940 while in Portsmouth. Transferred to the Free French. Decommissioned in December 1946.

6184.history: Captured by the Allies after the landings in North Africa. Disarmed in 1944. Finally stricken on 3 December 1945.

6185.history: Disarmed in October 1940. Stricken in April 1941. Further fate unknown.

6186.history: Seized by the British on 3 July 1940 while at Falmouth. Transferred to the Free French. Sunk by German aircraft during an air raid on Falmouth on 14 April 1941.

6187.history: Scuttled at Toulon on 27 November 1942 to prevent her capture by the Germans. Refloated by the Italians and renamed FR 53. Captured by the Germans on 9 September 1943 and renamed SG 21. Sunk by Allied aircraft on 23 November 1943, raised and again sunk by Allied aircraft on 15 August 1944.

6188.history: Seized by the British on 3 July 1940 while at Portsmouth. Transferred to the Free French (F.N.F.L.) on 3 September 1940. Served in the Pacific from mid 1941 until mid 1943. Sold to Tunisia in October 1959 being renamed Destur.

6189.history: Rejoined the Allies after the Allied invasion of North Africa. Transferred to Morocco in 1967.

6190.history: Sunk off Oran on 8 November 1942 by gunfire from the British destroyer HMS Brilliant.

6191.history: Captured by the Allies on 4 November 1942 and rejoined the Allied cause. Stricken 17 February 1953.

6192.history: Rejoined the Allies after the Allied landings in North Africa. Stricken on 18 October 1960.

6193.history: Seized by the British on 3 July 1940 while at Plymouth. Transferred to the Free French. Stricken in July 1963.

6194.history: Captured by the Italians at Bizerte, Tunisia on 8 December 1942 and renamed FR 52. Sunk at livorno, Italy by Allied aircraft on 28 May 1943. Raised and captured at Livorno by the Germans being renamed SG 22. Finally scuttled at Livorno in May 1945.

6195.history: Seized by the British on 3 July 1940 while at Plymouth. Transferred to the Free French (F.N.F.L.) on 26 July 1940. Stricken 18 August 1960.

6196.history: Interned in Turkey in July 1941. Returned to the French on 23 December 1944. Stricken 26 March 1958.

6197.history: Joined the Free French (F.N.F.L.) off Dakar on 23 September 1940. Stricken 8 March 1961.

6198.history: Captured on 8 December 1942 at Bizerte, Tunisia by the Italians and renamed FR 51. Scuttled by the Italians at Genoa, Italy on 9 September 1943 to prevent her capture by the Germans. Raised by the Germans and renamed SG 23. Scuttled by the Germans at Genoa on 24 April 1945.

6199.history: Joined the Allies after the Allied landing in North Africa. Stricken 15 April 1958.

6200.history: Seized by the British on 3 July 1940 while at Portsmouth. Operated by the Royal Navy until returned to France on 6 June 1945. Stricken in September 1964.

6201.history: Scuttled at Toulon on 27 November 1942 to prevent her capture by the Germans. Raised by the Italians on 6 April 1943 and renamed FR 55. Captured by the Germans on 9 September 1943 and renamed SG 25. Sunk on 15 August 1944 at Toulon during an Allied air raid.

6202.history: Joined the Allies after the Allied landing in North Africa. Stricken 11 September 1958.

6203.history: Seized by the British on 3 July 1940. Transferred to the Free French (F.N.F.L.) on 10 August 1940. Stricken 30 October 1965.

6204.history: Scuttled at Toulon on 27 November 1942 to prevent her capture by the Germans. Refloated by the Italians in 1943 and renamed FR 54. Captured by the Germans on 9 September 1943 and finally scuttled at Marseilles on 7 August 1944.

6205.history: Decommissioned 4 October 1945. Stricken 24 October 1945. Transferred to the Maritime Commission for disposal on 22 September 1945. Sold to Societe Anonima Navigazione Toscana at Livorno, Italy (Cameli group). Rebuilt in Italy as cargo and passengers vessel from 1949 to 1951, and was in service between the continent (Italy) and the islands of the Tuscan Archipelago (Elba, Capraia, Gorgona and Pianosa), from 1953 until 1972. The ship was renamed Rio Marina (a small town on Elba) and than changed in Portoferraio (the major town of Elba Island).
commanders: Hugh Mcleod Godsey, USNR (10 Dec 1942 - (1))

6206.history: Transferred to the Royal Canadian Navy at Devonport on 19 October 1939 and renamed HMCS Assiniboine.
commanders: Capt. Clifford Caslon, RN (23 Aug 1939 - 20 Sep 1939)
Lt.Cdr. Charles Eric Glasfurd, RN (20 Sep 1939 - 19 Oct 1939)

6207.built_by: Fore River Shipbuilding Co. (Quincy, Massachusetts, U.S.A.)
6207.history: Transferred by the U.S.N. to the Royal Navy on 23 September 1940. Collided with HMS Georgetown on 1 October 1940 while they were enroute to Britain. The ship headed back to St.John's, Newfoundland and was further damaged by grounding. After repairs she was commissioned by the Royal Canadian Navy on 6 July 1941.
commanders: Cdr. Lancelot Milman Shadwell, RN (23 Sep 1940 - Nov 1940)

6208.history: Scuttled by own crew at Tønsberg.

6209.built_by: Mare Island Navy Yard (Vallejo, California, U.S.A.)
6209.history: Captured at Horten by the Germans.

6210.history: Surrendered to the Germans at Fiskå. In German service as UC-1.

6211.history: Scuttled by own crew at Tønsberg.

6212.history: Escaped to Great Britain after the invasion of Norway.Decommissioned in 1944.

6215.history: Heavy damaged in North Sea by collision on 26 March 1945, not repaired. Sold 16 January 1947.

6217.history: HMS Kittiwake is not listed as active unit in the July 1945 Navy List Sold to China on 30 September 1946.
commanders: Cdr. Edward Reignier Conder, RN (5 Jan 1939 - late 1939)
Lt.Cdr. Kenneth Graham Tucker, OBE, RNR (7 Dec 1940 - 1 Mar 1942)

6219.history: Mined in the Humber estuary on 10 June 1941 some 28 nautical miles east-south-east of Spurn Point in position 53º30'536"N, 00º52'625"E. On 10 June 1941, the Harwich based patrol vessel HMS Pintail was escorting a convoy near 62-Buoy some 30 miles off the Humber when the steamship Royal Scot detonated an acoustic mine, blew up and sank. Pintail immediately dashed to the scene to help in the rescue, but she was also caught out by an acoustic mine, close to the steamship. HMS Pintail blew up and was lost almost immediately, instantly killing her Commanding Officer, Lt. John Leopold Elphinstone McClintock, RN, six-officers and forty-eight ratings. Lt.Cdr. D.J.L. Heber-Percy, RN, Commanding Officer of HMS Quantock, a destroyer also based at Harwich, which was passing with another convoy, reported later, "It was the most dreadful sight that I had ever seen. It did not seem possible that there could be any survivors". HMS Quantock and HNoMS Sleipner however, did manage to rescue one-third of her crew, twenty-two in all.

Lt. (retired) John Henry Atkinson, RN (1 Nov 1940 - 5 Feb 1941)

6222.built_by: Pullman Standard Car Co. (Chicago, Illinois, U.S.A.)
6222.history: Laid up in reserve at Sheerness, England in June 1945. Returned to the U.S.N on 10 December 1946. Sold in 1947 to Det Stavangerske Dampskibsselskab, Stavanger, Norway and rebuilt as a coastal passanger ship and renamed Haugesund. Sold in 1973 to Libera Navigazione Lauro S.A.S., Naples, Italy and renamed Sicilia Ponte. Sold in 1982 to Fila Societa per Azioni, Italy and renamed Tucalif. Scrapped in Italy in August 1997.
commanders: T/Lt. Eric Rawdon Humphrey Seddon, RNVR (Nov 1943 - Aug 1944)
T/Lt. Charles Trevor Letts, RNVR (Aug 1944 - )

6223.built_by: Pullman Standard Car Co. (Chicago, Illinois, U.S.A.)
6223.history: Returned USN in December 1946. Sold in 1947 to A/S KDS/Mosvald Shipping, Farsund, Norway. Rebuilt as a coastal passenger ship and renamed M/S Jylland. Sold in 1967 to E. Zammit and Co., Valetta, Malta. Sold in 1984 to a firm at Kibtur, Turkey and renamed Kibris. Sold again in 1984 to Akdeniz, Gemicilik, Turkey and renamed Akdeniz. Sold in 1986 to Charterwall Maritima S. A. , Pireus, Greece and renamed Princ.
commanders: Lt. Henry Owen L’Estrange, DSC, RNR (Nov 1943 - 14 Aug 1944)
A/Lt.Cdr. John Allan Dale, RNR (14 Aug 1944 - )

6224.built_by: Pullman Standard Car Co. (Chicago, Illinois, U.S.A.)
6224.history: Returned U.S.N. in December 1946. Sold to Norway and converted into a passenger ferry being renamed Stavanger. Sold to Finland and renamed Kong Sverre, later renamed Capri Corne. Scrapped in Finland.
commanders: T/Lt. David Malcolm Edwards, DSC, RNR (Nov 1943 - Oct 1944)
T/Lt. James Ralph Brown, RNR (Oct 1944 - )

6225.built_by: Pullman Standard Car Co. (Chicago, Illinois, U.S.A.)
6225.history: Returned U.S.N. in December 1946. Sold to Norway, converted to a passenger ferry and renamed Sunnhordland. Sold to the Kristina Cruise Line in Finland and renamed Kristina Brahe.
commanders: T/Lt. Ingram Ord Capper, RNVR (Nov 1943 - )

6226.built_by: Pullman Standard Car Co. (Chicago, Illinois, U.S.A.)
6226.history: Returned U.S.N. in December 1946. Sold into merchant service in 1947 and renamed Rio Vouga. Sold in 1979 to a Danish company and renamed Exportrader.
commanders: Lt. Kenneth James Hilton Wilkins, RANVR (16 Dec 1943 - )

6227.built_by: Pullman Standard Car Co. (Chicago, Illinois, U.S.A.)
6227.history: Returned U.S.N. in December 1946. Sold to Fager Lines, Norway in 1947 and converted to a passenger boat in 1949 being renamed M/S Sunnfjord.Laid up in Bergen in June 1973. Renamed Sunnfjord II in 1978. Sank while under tow to new owners in Oslo in october 1983.
commanders: T/Lt. Peter Pannell, RNVR (Nov 1943 - )

6228.built_by: Pullman Standard Car Co. (Chicago, Illinois, U.S.A.)
6228.history: Returned U.S.N. in 1946. Sold to Fylkesbaatane i Sogn og Fjordane, Bergen, Norway in 1949 and converted to a passenger ship being renamed M/S Sognefjord in 1950. Sold in 1982 to Filmeffekt A/S, Oslo, Norway and renamed Orion. Sold in 1984 to A/S Orion, Milde, Norway and laid up. Sold in 1987 to Matkat OY, Helsingfors, Finland. Sold again in 1991 to Orion Risteilyt O/Y, Hamina, Finland and renamed Orion II. Sold in 1996 to Jaako Mathias Eriksson of Honduras. Sold again in 1997 to an unknown buyer in Thailand and renamed Explorer. Currently (2004) named Orient Explorer.
commanders: T/Lt. Allen James Southgate, RNR (Nov 1943 - May 1944)
Lt. Brian Henry Fairley, RN (May 1944 - mid/late44)
T/Lt. Ronald Kenneth Palmer, RNVR (mid/late44 - late 1944)
T/Lt. Innes Athole Thorpe, RNVR (late 1944 - )

6229.built_by: Pullman Standard Car Co. (Chicago, Illinois, U.S.A.)
6229.history: Laid up in reserve in July 1945 at Sheerness, England. Returned to the U.S.N. on 10 December 1946. Sold to the Armand Pittman Co. in December 1946. Sold in 1948 to Giertsen and Co., Bergen, Norway and converted to a cargo ship being renamed M/S Nadodd. Sold in 1952 to Southern Lines Ltd. in the Phillipines and renamed General Wright. Sold in 1967 to the Sweet Line, Philippines and renamed Sweet Sail. Scrapped at Manila in April 1978.
commanders: T/Lt. Barry Cross Sheen, RNVR (Nov 1943 - )

6244.history: On 20 November 1939 HMT Mastiff (Lt.Cdr. Aymé Arthur Carrington Ouvry, RN) was engaged in the recovery of a mine, which had broken loose from a field laid on the night of 18/11 by German DDs Wilhelm Heidkamp, Hermann Künne and Bernd von Arnim. In the process of hauling it aboard, the mine detonated, destroying the trawler. 5 crew are missing presumed dead, 1 died of wounds and a further 9 are wounded.

6259.built_by: Henry Robb Ltd. (Leith, U.K.) : Whites M.E.
6259.history: Lost in a collision in the Morey Firth on 5 July 1942. During the early hours of the morning, whilst acting as escort to the east coast convoy WN.5, HMS Sword Dance (T/Lt. James Joseph Allon, RNR) was rammed in dense fog by one of merchants of the convoy, the Thyra-II (1088 GRT). The starboard coalbunker was holed in, the engine room rapidly flooded and HMS Sword Dance sank in less than an hour.

6262.built_by: Smith's Dock Co., Ltd. (South Bank-on-Tees, U.K.)
6262.history: Sold in July 1946 to Belgian owners. Converted in 1948 to fishing trawler at Chantier Naval St. Pierre, Hemiksem, Belgium. Dimensions 49,78m, 8,40m, 4,41 m Displacement 452 GRT 165 NRT 515 tdw. Sold to Compagnie Maritime du Progés, Ostende, Belgium in 1949 being renamed Ramspkapelle (callsign OPMP). Sold to Köser & Co., Hamburg, Germany in March 1952 being renamed Carola (callsign DECY). Sold to Anglo-Skandia G.m.b.H., Cuxhaven, Germany later in 1952. Laid up for about a year. Sold to Tankreederei Rhein-Weser G.m.b.H., Bremen, Germany in May 1953. Managed by DDG Hansa, Bremen, Germany, from 1956 the ship was managed by its owner. Reconstructed to Winetanker at the Jadewerft, Wilhelmshaven, Germany. Also a new engine was built in. New dimensions were 54,00m, 8,46m, 4,48m. New displacement was 488 GRT, 280 NRT, 649 tdw. Machinery 8-Cylinder Diesel engine (Maschinenbau Kiel A. G., Kiel-Friedrichsort) 600 PSe / 290 rpm, 1 shaft, 1 rudder, speed 11,0 kn. The ship was delivered in October 1953 being named Vinum (callsign DECY). Sold to Vinum S.p.A., Palermo, Italy in July 1962 being renamed Vino (Callsign IVJO) (LR-Nr. 5381459). Sold to Finnavi S.r.L., Napels, Italy in 1987. As of 1995/1996 no longer listed in Lloyd's Register of Shipping.
commanders: T/Lt. John Hunter, RNR (21 Jan 1941 - mid 43)
T/Lt. Robert Frederick Giles, RNVR (mid 43 - )

6270.built_by: Cochrane & Sons Shipbuilders Ltd. (Selby, U.K.) : Amos & Smith
6270.history: HMT Herring was sunk in a collision with the French merchant Cassard 2 nautical miles north of #20 E Buoy, north-east of Blyth. The wreck has been dived and lies intact and upright at depth of 60m. Both guns are still in place. There is a large dent at the stern where collision must have taken place.
